summary: The concluding Yom Kippur ritual: the bull (offered for the HP and priesthood) and the slaughtered goat (offered for Israel) are carried outside Jerusalem to the ash-disposal site (beit ha-deshen) and burned there. Those who carry them become ritually impure and must immerse in a mikveh and wait for sundown before returning. This burning is the physical conclusion of the Yom Kippur sacrifice complex, with all atonement services now complete.

display.whats: The bull and goat are carried out and burned at the ash-disposal site; carriers become impure
display.hint: The Mishnah records the final Yom Kippur service: the bull and goat are carried outside Jerusalem to the ash-disposal site and burned. Those who carry them become ritually impure and must immerse before they can return.

learning.summary: The final ritual act of Yom Kippur: the bull (offered for the HP and priesthood) and the goat (the slaughtered one, for Israel) must be carried out of Jerusalem to the place where ash is disposed of and burned there. Those who perform this task become ritually impure through contact with the burning animals. They immerse in a mikveh and wait for sundown before returning to Jerusalem. This burning is the physical conclusion of the Yom Kippur sacrifice complex.
learning.ahaMoment: The animals that purified the most sacred space must be taken to the most impure location (the ash heap) and burned - the holiest and most impure places are connected through these animals on this one day.
learning.memoryAnchor: The Yom Kippur animals make a complete journey: from the Temple's innermost to the city's outermost - from sacred center to impure periphery.
learning.openQuestion: Why do those who burn the animals become impure? What does this teach about handling the holiest sacrifices?

theological: the journey of the holiest animals to the most impure location

requiresUnderstanding: Knowing the Yom Kippur bull and goat are unique in being burned outside Jerusalem
Knowing that contact with certain burned offerings conveys impurity
misconceptions: The burning outside Jerusalem is a halacha that applies to these specific animals, not all offerings

visualizableElements.name: carriers taking bull and goat outside Jerusalem

quizSeeds.seed: What happens to the Yom Kippur bull and goat after all the services are complete?
quizSeeds.answer: They are carried outside Jerusalem to the ash-disposal site and burned there. Those who carry them become ritually impure and must immerse and wait for sundown before returning.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Yom Kippur bull and goat are burned outside Jerusalem; those who carry them become impure and must immerse afterward.

rashiTranslations.en: The bull and goat are burned at the place of the ashes.
The burning is at the place where other sin-offering animals are burned.
Those who carry them out become impure.
After immersing and waiting for sundown, they return.
The burning concludes the physical processing of the Yom Kippur offerings.
After burning: no more formal obligations for the day.
This is the final act of the Yom Kippur ritual complex.
The day ends with all obligations fulfilled.
The community waits for nightfall to break the fast.
The end of Yom Kippur: a return to normal life after the most intense day.
The fast has accomplished its purpose.
Israel stands before God after a full day of service, prayer, and fasting.
A new year begins with a clean slate.
